subjectLine: Shingles vaccine cuts cardiac mortality by 66% 💊
borisTake: The shingles vaccine data is the kind of finding that makes me want to call every patient over 50 immediately — a 66% reduction in all-cause mortality isn't a small effect size, it's transformative. Shingrix should be as standard as statins in any longevity protocol.
leadAnalysis: The ACC study analyzed 123,411 atherosclerotic patients over 50, matched for demographics, comparing those who received at least one dose of Shingrix or Zostavax against unvaccinated controls. The mechanism appears to be twofold: preventing VZV reactivation reduces prothrombotic inflammation directly, while trained immunity effects may also reduce chronic inflammaging more broadly. The 46% reduction in MACE and 66% reduction in all-cause mortality — comparable to smoking cessation — signals this isn't just antiviral protection but genuine cardiometabolic reprogramming. The 8-year cardioprotective window from prior data suggests the effect is durable, not just acute.
oneNumber: 66% | Reduction in all-cause mortality in atherosclerotic patients who received the shingles vaccine (n=123,411)

papers.title: Shingles Vaccine Slashes Cardiac Events by 46% and All-Cause Mortality by 66% in Atherosclerotic Patients
papers.summary: A 123,411-patient matched cohort study found that shingles vaccination was associated with 46% fewer major adverse cardiac events, 32% fewer heart attacks, 25% fewer strokes, 25% less heart failure, and 66% lower all-cause mortality in people over 50 with atherosclerotic disease. Effect sizes are comparable to smoking cessation.

papers.title: Humans with MSTN Loss-of-Function Variants Have 10%+ More Muscle Mass and Less Adiposity
papers.summary: Using data from 1.1 million individuals including deep-learning MRI body composition analysis of 77,572 UK Biobank participants, researchers confirmed that heterozygous MSTN loss-of-function carriers show >10% increased muscle mass across multiple muscle groups with decreased adiposity. Provides the largest human genetic evidence yet for long-term myostatin blockade safety and efficacy.

papers.title: Aβ Displaces Tau from Microtubules — A New Unifying Theory for Alzheimer's Pathology
papers.summary: Researchers propose that amyloid-β binds directly to microtubules with affinity comparable to tau itself, physically displacing tau and triggering phosphorylation and aggregation. This model explains the inconsistencies between amyloid burden and cognitive decline, and shifts therapeutic focus toward protecting the microtubule-tau interaction rather than Aβ clearance alone.

papers.title: In Vivo CRISPR-Created CAR T Cells Eliminate Tumors Across Three Cancer Types in Mice
papers.summary: UC San Francisco researchers combined enveloped delivery vehicles (EDVs) and AAV vectors to create CAR T cells in vivo by inserting a CAR gene at the TRAC locus via CRISPR — bypassing the 3-5 week, $500K+ ex vivo manufacturing process. The approach showed higher efficacy than conventional CAR T cells across three cancer types including a solid tumor.

papers.title: Gut Bacterium Roseburia inulinivorans Linked to 29% Higher Handgrip Strength in Older Adults
papers.summary: Researchers found that older adults with detectable Roseburia inulinivorans in their stool had 29% higher handgrip strength, confirmed in both human cohorts and mice. The finding raises the prospect of microbiome-targeted exercise mimetics — particularly relevant as GLP-1 agonists drive lean mass loss at scale.

papers.title: Partial Reprogramming Is Conserved from Insect to Mammal
papers.summary: A new study demonstrates that the rejuvenating effects of partial reprogramming are conserved across species from insects to mammals, suggesting deep evolutionary roots for epigenetic age reversal mechanisms. This cross-species validation strengthens the mechanistic basis for reprogramming-based longevity therapies.

papers.title: 7-Ketocholesterol: A Comprehensive Review of Its Systemic Toxicity and Therapeutic Targeting
papers.summary: Matthew O'Connor of Cyclarity Therapeutics co-authored a review detailing the wide-ranging damage caused by 7-ketocholesterol (7KC), an oxidized cholesterol accumulating with age that drives atherosclerosis, neurodegeneration, and cellular senescence. The review builds the case for 7KC clearance as a therapeutic target and highlights the cyclodextrin-based approach in development.
